title: Platform Operator Lifecycle Automation Cookbook
preview:
Summary:
This cookbook describes a standardized, automation-first approach to the platform operator lifecycle at Redwood Inference. It covers operator onboarding, preflight validations, automated certification and proctoring, staged change rollouts, offboarding, and continuous skills/knowledge validation. The goal i...
text:
Summary:
This cookbook describes a standardized, automation-first approach to the platform operator lifecycle at Redwood Inference. It covers operator onboarding, preflight validations, automated certification and proctoring, staged change rollouts, offboarding, and continuous skills/knowledge validation. The goal is to reduce manual handoffs, compress time-to-competency, and ensure consistent operator responses during incidents and maintenance windows.

Scope and audience:
- Scope: Platform and SRE operator lifecycle activities that touch the platform operator surface (prefetch/cache operators, deployment operators, capacity operators, regional failover).
- Audience: Platform Engineering (primary), Eng-SRE, Team Leads, Hiring/People Ops (secondary).

Goals:
1. Automate repetitive operator onboarding tasks so new operators reach certified readiness within 10 business days.
2. Guarante...

title: Cost-aware SLI Degradation Incident Response Playbook
preview:
Overview:

This playbook describes detection, triage, and mitigation steps for incidents where service-level indicators (SLIs) degrade due to cost-driven behavior (e.g., aggressive autoscaling, batch sizing, routing to high-cost capacity) rather than pure software faults. It documents practical on-call actions, deci...
text:
Overview:

This playbook describes detection, triage, and mitigation steps for incidents where service-level indicators (SLIs) degrade due to cost-driven behavior (e.g., aggressive autoscaling, batch sizing, routing to high-cost capacity) rather than pure software faults. It documents practical on-call actions, decision criteria for manual intervention, and post-incident follow-up requirements.

Purpose:
- Reduce time-to-mitigation for SLI degradation caused or amplified by cost controls.
- Standardize operator decisions when balancing latency, throughput, and spend.
- Provide reproducible mitigation steps that preserve customer-facing availability and reduce unexpected billing exposure.

Scope:
- Applies to Redwood API and Dedicated serving surfaces (hosted endpoints, model routing, GPU pools).
- Targets degradations where cost signals (billing spikes, quota enforcement, eviction eve...

title: Observability cost-sandboxing pilot retrospective
preview:
Summary:

This retrospective covers the Observability Cost-Sandboxing Pilot (Sep 2026 — Nov 2026). The pilot tested a lightweight sandboxing workflow for experimental telemetry consumers to run new metrics/alerts in a cost-isolated environment before promoting them to production. Goals: reduce unreviewed telemetry s...
text:
Summary:

This retrospective covers the Observability Cost-Sandboxing Pilot (Sep 2026 — Nov 2026). The pilot tested a lightweight sandboxing workflow for experimental telemetry consumers to run new metrics/alerts in a cost-isolated environment before promoting them to production. Goals: reduce unreviewed telemetry sprawl, measure actual cost impact of new instrumentation, and shorten feedback loops for SLI owners.

Background:

- Problem statement:
 - Over the past 18 months we onboarded ~120 new telemetry consumers (dashboards, alerts, continuous guards). Many were created without explicit ownership or cost estimates.
 - Result: increasing ingestion bills, alert noise, and SLI signal duplication across teams.

- Pilot hypothesis:
 1) A sandboxed pathway (dev -> sandbox -> prod) with cost accounting and a lightweight review gate will reduce unnecessary production telemetry by >= 30% f...

title: billing-integrity-synthetic-tenant-suite-2026
preview:
Overview
========
This document defines the Billing Integrity Synthetic Tenant Suite (BISTS): a canonical set of synthetic tenant workloads, dashboards, alerts, and operator runbooks used to validate end-to-end billing and chargeback fidelity for Redwood platform releases and capacity/topology changes. The suite is ...
text:
Overview
========
This document defines the Billing Integrity Synthetic Tenant Suite (BISTS): a canonical set of synthetic tenant workloads, dashboards, alerts, and operator runbooks used to validate end-to-end billing and chargeback fidelity for Redwood platform releases and capacity/topology changes. The suite is intended for platform engineers, SREs, and finance engineering to detect regressions in token accounting, multi-tenant attribution, and billing pipeline transformations before customer impact.

Why this exists
----------------
- Past incidents have exposed mismatches between runtime token attribution (inference runtime) and downstream billing pipelines (ingest, aggregation, invoice generation).
- We need a reproducible, automated way to: (a) generate representative charge patterns, (b) exercise telemetry and ingestion paths, and (c) reconcile expected vs actual charged unit...

Overview

Purpose: This page documents a repeatable lab for reproducing and triaging emergent platform behaviors (e.g., cross-tenant KV cache storms, prefix-cache thrash, inter-model tail-latency cascades) and the dashboard artifacts operators should use during exercises and incidents. The lab is designed for platform operators, SREs, and reliability engineers who run postmortems, runbooks, and training.

Scope: covers simulation recipes, dashboard templates, runbook fragments, operator checklists, curriculum for training sessions, and a short roadmap for expanding the lab. This is not a step-by-step system upgrade guide; see the platform runbooks for those procedures.

Goals
- Provide deterministic, reproducible scenarios that manifest realistic emergent failure modes in the runtime.
- Produce operator dashboard views that surface root-cause signals quickly.

title: Batching Defaults: Observability Dashboard + Alert Spec (SLO Tiers)

This spec is written for:

text:
## Overview
This page specifies the dashboards and alerts required for the tiered “workload-aware batching defaults” rollout (fast / standard / cost-efficient). The goal is to make tier behavior visible, explainable, and safe to operate under production traffic.

This spec is written for:
- Runtime / Scheduling: validate scheduler behavior and diagnose tail latency regressions.
- SRE: set actionable alerts tied to SLO tiers and overload behavior.
- Platform / Console: power “effective tier” and “why this profile” views.

## Goals
1) Provide per-tier latency visibility (histograms + percentiles) for both streaming and non-streaming traffic.
2) Surface scheduler health: queue depth, wait time, admission control/load shedding, and starvation indicators.
3) Validate economics: throughput, batching efficiency, and cache hit rate.
4) Detect regressions quickly: canary vs baseline comparison...

title: Cross-region signal fusion and fallback runbook
preview:
Summary
-------
This runbook describes how platform operators should interpret fused latency and cost signals across regions, escalate, and perform safe fallbacks for LLM inference traffic. It codifies signal sources, fusion heuristics, alert thresholds, operator actions, and automated fallback configuration. The go...
text:
Summary
-------
This runbook describes how platform operators should interpret fused latency and cost signals across regions, escalate, and perform safe fallbacks for LLM inference traffic. It codifies signal sources, fusion heuristics, alert thresholds, operator actions, and automated fallback configuration. The goal is to preserve customer-facing SLOs (p95 latency < 300ms for interactive routes; token cost drift alerts at > +30% delta) while limiting unnecessary cross-region failovers and cost overruns.

Scope
-----
- Services in scope: runtime-gateway, batch-router, kv-prefix-cache, regional-allocator, traffic-shepherd.
- Regions: us-west-2, us-east-1, eu-west-1, ap-southeast-1.
- Applies to hosted and dedicated deployments; private deployments may adapt the heuristics but should follow the same escalation flow.

Signal sources and priorities
----------------------------
1) Latency...

title: Service poison-pill safety and isolation guide
preview:
Summary
-------
This guide defines detection, automated mitigation, and operator isolation procedures for "poison-pill" requests and workloads in the Redwood inference platform. A poison-pill is any request or workload pattern that rapidly escalates resource usage or error propagation and risks cross-tenant/service ...
text:
Summary
-------
This guide defines detection, automated mitigation, and operator isolation procedures for "poison-pill" requests and workloads in the Redwood inference platform. A poison-pill is any request or workload pattern that rapidly escalates resource usage or error propagation and risks cross-tenant/service degradation (examples: pathological prompts that exhaust KV caches, malformed inputs that trigger infinite loops in model runtime, or tenant-driven token-surges).

Scope & applicability
---------------------
Applies to: inference-gateway, batching-worker, kv-prefix-cache, model-eval-sandbox, and the routing layer (router and model-variant-fallbacker). Does NOT replace tenant-level security policies; complements quota and billing controls.

Goals
-----
- Detect poison-pill behavior quickly with deterministic signals.
- Isolate affected tenants/requests before systemic impact...

title: Optimization Config diff engine + semantic validation (2026-02)
preview:
Overview
Optimization Configs are immutable, versioned JSON payloads used by the control plane to drive runtime behavior (batching, caching, and quantization). To make safe apply/rollback operationally safe, we maintain:
1) a deterministic, human-friendly diff between two versions, and
2) semantic validators that de...
text:
Overview
Optimization Configs are immutable, versioned JSON payloads used by the control plane to drive runtime behavior (batching, caching, and quantization). To make safe apply/rollback operationally safe, we maintain:
1) a deterministic, human-friendly diff between two versions, and
2) semantic validators that detect dangerous or invalid changes before rollout.

This page documents the current (2026-02) diff format, validation semantics, and rule catalog used by:
- Optimization Config Service (/validate and /apply)
- Console diff view and gating UX
- Rollout controller policy decisions

What changed since the original ENG-4822 implementation
- Batching bounds are now capability-aware: the control plane applies conservative global limits AND enforces per-hardware maxima from runtime capability snapshots.
- Cache shard limits were increased (256 -> 512) to support higher-parallelism ...

text:
## Overview
This page defines the Grafana dashboards and alerting rules used for the FP8/INT8 kernel benchmarking sprint and for staged rollout monitoring (perf canary → expanded cohorts). It standardizes:
- What we measure (TTFT, TPOT, throughput, errors, GPU health, scheduler signals)
- How we slice results (model/GPU/region/kernel-mode/workload)
- How we alert (thresholds, noise suppression, sample-size rules, routing)

**Primary consumers:** Serving Runtime (Kernel + Scheduling), SRE, and Infra during benchmarking and rollout.

**Out of scope:** Customer-facing charts/claims. See Product guidance and benchmarking standards for what can be externally shared.

---

## Goals and non-goals
### Goals
1) Make benchmark runs comparable and debuggable (kernel wins vs scheduler/queuing artifacts).
2) Provide fast rollback signals during canary (TTFT/TPOT regressions, error spikes, GPU memo...

title: Cost Model API Observability (Dashboards + Alerts)

This page defines the required dashboards and alerting for the **Cost Model API** (internal service) used by Console and Optimize. The intent is to:
- Keep Console hot paths reliable (bounded latency + bounded error rates).
- Provide early warning on correctness issues (cost drift vs observed spend) and i...
text:
## Purpose
This page defines the required dashboards and alerting for the **Cost Model API** (internal service) used by Console and Optimize. The intent is to:
- Keep Console hot paths reliable (bounded latency + bounded error rates).
- Provide early warning on correctness issues (cost drift vs observed spend) and input data health (telemetry lag, schema/version mismatches).
- Make schema adoption visible (distribution by schema_version and client build/version) to support safe deprecations.

Non-goals:
- This is not a billing/invoicing monitoring spec. Billing systems may reuse primitives, but Cost Model is not the system of record.

## Service + ownership
**Service name:** cost-model-service

**Primary oncall/ownership:** Platform Control Plane (Ethan Park) 
**Reliability partner:** SRE (Rafael Mendes)

**Critical clients:**
- Console backend (Chloe Martin / Jada Williams)
- Optimiz...

title: Runbook: Auto-rollback on SLO breach (triage, override, restart rollout)
preview:

This runbook describes how to respond when Redwood’s rollout controller **automatically pauses or rolls back** a rollout due to an SLO breach (latency/error-rate/saturation). It covers:
- How to confirm what happened (pause vs rollback vs suppressed)

text:
## Purpose
This runbook describes how to respond when Redwood’s rollout controller **automatically pauses or rolls back** a rollout due to an SLO breach (latency/error-rate/saturation). It covers:
- How to confirm what happened (pause vs rollback vs suppressed)
- How to validate monitor correctness (missing, stale, mis-scoped, bad labels)
- How to identify the likely culprit (model version, runtime config, region, tier)
- How to use admin overrides/suppression safely (with auditability)
- How to restart a rollout without re-triggering flapping

This is intended for **on-call SREs**, **service owners**, and **platform/console on-call**.

---

## Quick glossary
- **Rollout**: staged traffic shift from baseline → candidate (canary or A/B), possibly across regions/tiers.
- **Auto action**: controller decision taken due to SLO evaluation. One of:
 - **PAUSE**: stop progressing rollout step...

title: Kernel Selection v2 Rollout Dashboards (Spec)
preview:

This page specifies the dashboards and alerting required to safely roll out **Kernel auto-selection v2** across Hosted, Dedicated, and (where applicable) Private deployments.

The rollout dashboards must answer four questions quickly:
1) **What is v2 choosing?** (selection distribution by model/GPU/sequen...
text:
## Purpose
This page specifies the dashboards and alerting required to safely roll out **Kernel auto-selection v2** across Hosted, Dedicated, and (where applicable) Private deployments.

The rollout dashboards must answer four questions quickly:
1) **What is v2 choosing?** (selection distribution by model/GPU/sequence regime)
2) **Is it faster?** (p95/p99 latency deltas, throughput stability)
3) **Is it safe?** (error rates, fallback behavior, determinism/correctness guardrails)
4) **When do we roll back?** (clear trigger thresholds + automated guidance)

Non-goals:
- This page does not document how the selector works internally (see design doc/ADR).
- This page does not replace the oncall runbook; it defines what the runbook relies on.

---

## Audience
- Runtime oncall, SRE oncall, Release Engineering during the runtime 1.21 train
- Runtime Kernels + Scheduling teams validating roll...

text:
## Purpose
This runbook covers triage and mitigation for elevated latency and/or 5xx error rate on the **Suggestions API** used by the Redwood Console “Optimization suggestions” beta.

Primary goals:
- Restore availability and latency SLOs quickly.
- Prevent cross-tenant or broad Console impact (UI timeouts, repeated retries).
- Preserve correctness guarantees (RBAC + org/project scoping) while degraded.

Non-goals:
- Deep root cause analysis (capture data for follow-up; open an incident or ticket as needed).

---

## Service / Endpoint Overview
**User-facing surface:** Redwood Console → Optimize → Suggestions

**Backend service:** `optimize-suggestions-service`

**Primary endpoint (internal naming):**
- `GET /optimize/suggestions` (list; paginated, sortable)
- `GET /optimize/suggestions/{suggestion_id}` (details)

**Common query parameters:**
- `org_id`, `project_id`
- `route_id` (op...

## Overview
This page specifies the Grafana dashboards and alerts used to monitor the **Optimization Suggestions (Beta)** user funnel in the Redwood Console.

Primary goal: make it easy to answer:
- Are customers seeing suggestions (impressions)?
- Are they engaging (expands) and/or rejecting (dismissals)?
- Are they taking action (apply clicks / deep links to config pages)?
- Are there cohort differences by **org, project, model, route, and region**?
- Is telemetry/data freshness good enough to trust the funnel?

This spec covers **UI funnel telemetry** and supporting health signals (API + compute + ingestion lag). It intentionally does **not** attempt to measure realized savings; that belongs in billing/cost attribution reporting.

## Audience

title: Optimize Suggestions Service (Batching/Caching) — Service Overview
preview:
## Summary
The **Optimize Suggestions Service** produces actionable, conservative recommendations to reduce inference cost for Redwood customers. In the current beta scope, the service generates suggestions for:
- **Batching configuration** (continuous batching / max batch tokens / queue delay)
- **Caching configura...
text:
## Summary
The **Optimize Suggestions Service** produces actionable, conservative recommendations to reduce inference cost for Redwood customers. In the current beta scope, the service generates suggestions for:
- **Batching configuration** (continuous batching / max batch tokens / queue delay)
- **Caching configuration** (prefix/KV caching enablement and tuning)

Suggestions are surfaced in the **Redwood Console → Optimize → Suggestions** and are designed to be:
- **Explainable** (clear “why” and “how to apply”)
- **Conservative** (avoid overpromising savings)
- **Safe** (risk labels + guardrails; no auto-apply)

This page describes architecture, data sources, compute cadence, interfaces, ownership, and failure modes.

---

## Scope and non-goals
### In scope (beta)
- Suggestion generation for batching and caching
- Savings estimates (cost delta, $/day and %)
- Performance impact est...

title: Rollout Controller Service Overview
preview:
## Overview
The **Rollout Controller** (service name: `rollout-controller`, internal shorthand: **RC**) is Redwood’s control-plane service responsible for orchestrating **staged model deployments** and **traffic-shift rollouts** (canary + A/B) for inference routes across Hosted, Dedicated, and Private environments.
...
text:
## Overview
The **Rollout Controller** (service name: `rollout-controller`, internal shorthand: **RC**) is Redwood’s control-plane service responsible for orchestrating **staged model deployments** and **traffic-shift rollouts** (canary + A/B) for inference routes across Hosted, Dedicated, and Private environments.

RC owns the rollout **state machine**, applies **policy guardrails**, coordinates with execution systems to change routing/weights, and records an **audit trail** for every step and automated action (pause/rollback/suppress).

This page is the operational/service reference for engineers and SREs: responsibilities, dependencies, failure modes, and on-call ownership.

---

## Responsibilities (what RC does)
### 1) Rollout orchestration
RC manages rollout plans for a scoped target:
- **Scope:** `service/route`, `model_version`, `region`, `customer_tier` (and optionally `custo...

title: How-to: Integrate Optimize with the Cost Model API (What-if projections + ROI)
preview:
## Purpose
This guide explains how Redwood Optimize should call the Cost Model API v1 “what-if” endpoints to produce consistent, defensible ROI and savings estimates across batching, caching, and quantization recommendations.

It covers:
- How to construct workload summaries (aggregated inputs) instead of per-reques...
text:
## Purpose
This guide explains how Redwood Optimize should call the Cost Model API v1 “what-if” endpoints to produce consistent, defensible ROI and savings estimates across batching, caching, and quantization recommendations.

It covers:
- How to construct workload summaries (aggregated inputs) instead of per-request payloads
- How to call the what-if projection endpoint(s) safely (bounds, timeouts, retries)
- How to interpret confidence intervals and communicate uncertainty
- Guardrails: when *not* to show ROI, and how to avoid over-optimistic recommendations

Optimize should treat the Cost Model API as the canonical source for cost primitives and projections. Avoid re-implementing cost math in Optimize except for UI presentation and product logic.

---

## Audience
- Optimize backend + recommendation pipeline engineers
- Applied ML (quantization / caching / batching ROI modeling)
- ...

text:
## Purpose
This runbook describes how to execute a **one-click rollback** of an Optimization Config version (batching, caching, and quantization profiles) for Hosted and Dedicated deployments, and how to validate recovery.

Rollback is intended to be:
- **Fast** (single action from Console or API)
- **Safe** (idempotent + race-condition guarded)
- **Auditable** (structured audit events)
- **Scoped** (tenant/model/region aware)

## Audience
- SRE on-call
- Eng Platform (Control Plane) on-call
- Runtime on-call
- Console on-call

## Definitions
- **Optimization Config**: Serving-time knobs (batching heuristics, prefix/KV cache toggles, quantization profile selection) applied by the runtime.
- **Config Version**: Immutable, versioned object (e.g., `ocv_2026_02_24_0017`).
- **Active Version**: The version currently pinned for a given scope (tenant/model/region).
- **Apply / Rollout**: Sta...

text:
## Purpose
This runbook describes how to respond when Redwood’s audit logging coverage regresses (e.g., a newly added/changed admin endpoint or workflow no longer emits a required audit event, or emits an event that fails schema/taxonomy requirements).

Coverage regressions are treated as security-impacting quality issues because they can break customer detection/investigation workflows and SOC2/ISO-aligned evidence expectations.

## When to use this runbook
Use this runbook when any of the following occurs:
- The Audit Coverage dashboard shows a drop in observed coverage for one or more `event_type`s.
- An alert fires for missing expected event types for a sensitive action.
- Support/Solutions reports a customer issue: “action happened, but we don’t see a corresponding audit event” (common in Private and Console workflows).
- CI begins failing due to new/changed code paths missing re...

title: Usage Insights Query Service (UIQS) — system and service doc
preview:
## Overview
The **Usage Insights Query Service (UIQS)** is the backend service that powers in-console cost/usage breakdowns by **API route** and **model** (and optional dimensions like workspace/project, region, and deployment mode). It provides a low-latency query API for interactive console tables and driver expla...
text:
## Overview
The **Usage Insights Query Service (UIQS)** is the backend service that powers in-console cost/usage breakdowns by **API route** and **model** (and optional dimensions like workspace/project, region, and deployment mode). It provides a low-latency query API for interactive console tables and driver explanations, while enforcing cost-visibility RBAC and protecting the telemetry warehouse from unbounded queries.

UIQS does **not** compute attribution itself. It queries pre-aggregated datasets produced by Telemetry & Analytics jobs and applies additional filtering, bucketing, pagination, and explainability rules.

### Primary consumers
- **Redwood Console** (Usage → Cost → Route/Model views)
- Internal tools for reconciliation/debugging (eng + support)
- Export job subsystem (async CSV generation; see API contract for lifecycle)

### Owners
- Product area: Console Insights (M...

text:
## Purpose
This page explains how to enable the **Optimization Suggestions (batching/caching) beta** for a specific customer org using feature flags.

This is an internal-only operational procedure used by Product/CS/Solutions and on-call engineering during beta rollout.

---

## What gets enabled
The beta is gated by **two controls**:

1) **Console UI flag** (shows the Suggestions entry point, list view, and details drawer)
- Flag key: `console.optimize_suggestions_beta`

2) **Backend API allowlist flag** (permits `/optimize/suggestions` reads for the org)
- Flag key: `optimize.suggestions_api_enabled`

Both flags must be enabled for the org to have a functional experience.

Why two flags?
- The API flag prevents accidental exposure via direct API calls.
- The UI flag allows gradual UX rollout and easy UI-only rollback without fully disabling the service.

---

text:
## Purpose
This runbook covers how to enable Shadow Traffic safely, how to monitor and control overhead, and how to disable/rollback quickly if the shadow path (routing, telemetry, scoring) degrades.

Shadow Traffic duplicates a sampled subset of production requests to one or more shadow targets (candidate variants) while returning the control response to the user. It must be **fail-open**: any shadow failures must not impact the user path.

**Primary owners**
- Data plane / routing: Mina Haddad / Ravi Menon / Vikram Singh
- Control plane policy: Ethan Park / Benji Okafor / Jordan Lee
- Telemetry pipelines: Logan Wright / Nadia Rahman / Caleb Johnson
- Scoring workers / eval-harness integration: Jonas Weber / Karthik Iyer / Olivia Grant
- SRE / alerting: Sean Gallagher / Allison Grant
- Security controls: Marcus Lin / Kevin Osei

title: Meter Sanity Dashboard + Alerting Spec (Billing Telemetry Data Quality)
preview:
## 1. Purpose
This page specifies the "Meter Sanity" dashboards and alerts used to continuously validate billing telemetry integrity and catch invoice-impacting issues early. The dashboards/alerts are used by Engineering (Telemetry + Platform), SRE on-call, and Finance/RevRec during month-end close.

The spec covers...
text:
## 1. Purpose
This page specifies the "Meter Sanity" dashboards and alerts used to continuously validate billing telemetry integrity and catch invoice-impacting issues early. The dashboards/alerts are used by Engineering (Telemetry + Platform), SRE on-call, and Finance/RevRec during month-end close.

The spec covers:
- Drift checks: aggregated usage events vs billing ledger (and vs customer usage exports where applicable)
- Data completeness: null/unknown dimension rates, missing required fields
- Uniqueness/idempotency: duplicate event rates, dedupe effectiveness signals
- Freshness: lag from request logs → usage events → ledger/export tables
- Breakdowns by account, model, region, endpoint, and deployment mode (Hosted API vs Dedicated)

Non-goals:
- Replacing Finance reconciliation procedures; this is early detection and triage tooling.

title: Shadow Traffic Policy Service (STPS) — Control Plane Service Overview
preview:
## Overview
Shadow Traffic Policy Service (STPS) is the control-plane service responsible for storing, validating, versioning, and distributing **shadow traffic policies** that mirror a sampled subset of production requests to one or more shadow targets.

STPS does **not** run inference and does **not** participate ...
text:
## Overview
Shadow Traffic Policy Service (STPS) is the control-plane service responsible for storing, validating, versioning, and distributing **shadow traffic policies** that mirror a sampled subset of production requests to one or more shadow targets.

STPS does **not** run inference and does **not** participate on the user request path directly. It produces a compiled policy artifact that the data plane (Smart Routing / Edge Router) consumes to make mirroring decisions deterministically and safely.

This document covers:
- Service responsibilities and boundaries
- API surface (internal + Console backend)
- Validation rules and guardrails
- Storage model and versioning
- Migrations and rollout considerations
- Integration points (routing, rollouts, telemetry, audit)

---

## Responsibilities
### In scope
- Persist shadow traffic policy configs attached to:
 - **Routes** (e.g., /v1/...

title: Cost Model Service (cost-model-api) — Service Overview
preview:
## Summary

It is the canonical source for:

text:
## Summary
Cost Model Service (service name: **cost-model-api**) provides a single, versioned implementation for inference cost estimation and cost breakdowns across Redwood products.

It is the canonical source for:
- **Per-request cost estimates** and standardized breakdown taxonomy (prefill/decode, cache, batch efficiency, overhead allocation, quantization deltas)
- **Aggregated cost breakdowns** for Console dashboards (bounded-cardinality responses)
- **What-if projections** used by Optimize to quantify ROI from configuration changes (batching/caching/quantization)

This document describes architecture, dependencies, SLOs, ownership/on-call, and rollout strategy.

---

## Service owners
**Primary owner team:** Platform Control Plane
- Service/API lead: Ethan Park
- Control plane integration: Benji Okafor, Cole Summers
- Auth/tenancy: Kaitlyn Nguyen

**Reliability partner:** SRE
- ...

## Purpose
This page documents the recommended integration pattern for **Redwood Console** backend services calling the **Cost Model API (v1)**.

Console uses the Cost Model API to power:
- Cost breakdown tables (prefill vs decode, cache hit attribution, overhead)
- Time-series dashboards (bounded, aggregated breakdowns)
- Drill-down views for a request or small cohort

The intent is that Console uses a **single canonical source** of cost semantics and breakdown taxonomy, rather than duplicating logic.

## Audience
- Console backend engineers and dashboard engineers
- On-call engineers debugging cost discrepancies in Console

title: Graceful Degradation Operator Templates and Runbook
preview:
Overview

This document provides operator-ready templates and a step-by-step runbook for enacting graceful degradation across Redwood's inference platform. It focuses on predictable, auditable actions that reduce tail risk (latency and error bursts) while preserving the highest-value traffic. The goal is to give on-...
text:
Overview

This document provides operator-ready templates and a step-by-step runbook for enacting graceful degradation across Redwood's inference platform. It focuses on predictable, auditable actions that reduce tail risk (latency and error bursts) while preserving the highest-value traffic. The goal is to give on-call operators a small set of reversible, low-risk levers with clear success criteria and dashboard panels to track outcomes.

When to use this runbook

1. Regional latency or 5xx spike that persists > 5 minutes and cannot be remediated by autoscaling.
2. GPU pool eviction / degraded capacity that reduces throughput below committed levels.
3. Cost shock or runaway token usage identified by cost dashboards with immediate financial impact.
4. Model quality regressions requiring temporary routing to safer variants.

Tiered degradation principles

- Safety-first: ensure availab...

title: Dashboard spec: Hot-route capacity protection (Hosted API + Dedicated)
preview:

The goal is to make overload protection behavior (rate limits, quotas, admission control, and controlled shedding) **observable, diagnosable, and supportable**, while ensuring we page o...
text:
## Summary
This document specifies the required dashboards and alerting rules for the **Hot-route capacity protection** initiative. The goal is to make overload protection behavior (rate limits, quotas, admission control, and controlled shedding) **observable, diagnosable, and supportable**, while ensuring we page only when **enterprise-protected routes** are at risk of missing SLOs.

This spec is written for Grafana + Prometheus/Tempo (metrics + tracing), but the panel semantics apply regardless of backend.

## Goals
1) Provide a single “is the fleet healthy?” view per region and per protected route group.
2) Make it easy for on-call to answer:
 - Are we shedding? Who is being shed (tier/route group) and why (quota vs overload vs admission control)?
 - Are enterprise SLOs holding while best-effort is throttled?
 - Is the problem at gateway enforcement, routing/admission control, or r...

## Overview
This page describes the operator workflow for applying an **Optimization Config** change safely using a **staged canary rollout**. It covers:
- Creating an immutable config version
- Validating schema + semantic constraints
- Running a canary (tenant/model/region scoped) with automated gates
- Promoting to 100% (or broader scope)
- Monitoring and documenting the change

This workflow applies to **Hosted** and **Dedicated** (managed by the Redwood control plane). For **Private** deployments, see the Private operator guide referenced under Related Pages.

---

## When to use this procedure
Use a canary rollout for any change that can affect latency/cost/reliability, including:
- Batching heuristics (max batch size, queue delay, target tokens/step)
- Cache toggles (prefix/KV cache enablement, cache size limits)
- Quantization profile changes

Summary:
This document is an operator-focused decision canvas and runbook for safely performing predictive prefetch/prefix-warmup and executing deferred rollbacks when aggressive prefetch policies cause SLO regressions or cost spikes. It synthesizes dashboard signals, alert thresholds, and step-by-step actions to guide oncall and operator teams through detection, verification, mitigation, rollback, and retrospective capture.

Scope and audience:

- Audience: platform operators, oncall engineers, ops SREs, release owners.

Why this exists:

Summary
This document defines a reproducible decomposition of platform capacity into orthogonal buckets (critical-path, best-effort, cacheable, ephemeral) and provides an SLI/SLO ownership matrix to map each bucket to teams, dashboards, alerting thresholds, and operational runbooks. The goal is to make capacity reasoning explicit for product and operator teams, enable cost-aware routing, and reduce cross-team incident ambiguity.

Scope
- Applies to Redwood Inference runtime components and platform control plane services that affect inference availability and throughput: router, request-router, kv-prefix-cache, batch-worker, model-proxy, autoscaler-controller, tenant-allocator.
- Covers hosted and dedicated fleets; private deployments are guidance-only (network/topology differences require local adaptation).

text:
## Overview
This page specifies the Grafana (and optionally Looker) dashboards and alerting used to report and triage results from the Q1 2026 “Perf baseline & representative workload suite” project.

The dashboards must support:
- Baseline reporting for key metrics (TTFT/TPOT percentiles, throughput)
- Root-cause oriented drilldowns (saturation, queueing, kernel selection, cache behavior)
- Run-to-run comparison and regression attribution
- Direct linking from dashboard → run manifest → traces/logs/artifacts

This spec is intentionally prescriptive: consistent naming, tags, and panel layout are required so that engineers can move between runs, models, regions, and SKUs without relearning the UI.

## Goals
1) Provide a single “source of truth” view for a benchmark run (or run set) including:
- TTFT p50/p95/p99
- TPOT p50/p95/p99
- Throughput (tokens/s, req/s) and concurrency
- Saturat...

title: Adaptive Prefetch Hint Layer: design, operator guide, and rollout
preview:
Summary
-------
This document describes the Adaptive Prefetch Hint Layer (APHL): a lightweight application-facing hinting protocol and runtime integration that lets upstream services provide controlled prefetch requests to the LLM runtime to reduce tail latency for common prefixes while preserving global SLOs and co...
text:
Summary
-------
This document describes the Adaptive Prefetch Hint Layer (APHL): a lightweight application-facing hinting protocol and runtime integration that lets upstream services provide controlled prefetch requests to the LLM runtime to reduce tail latency for common prefixes while preserving global SLOs and cost budgets. It includes design rationale, runtime components, operator playbooks, dashboarding requirements, and a safe rollout plan.

Intended audience
- Platform operators and on-call engineers
- Runtime and serving engineers
- Client SDK and API owners

Why we built APHL
- Observation: a small fraction of request patterns (e.g., prompt templates, system messages, high-frequency webhook patterns) dominate 75% of tail latency because early tokens are not hot in the prefix/KV cache.
- Goal: reduce p95/p99 generation latency for known safe prefixes without increasing token c...

- Scope: Platform control plane, serving runtime (GPU pools, batching layer, KV/prefix cache), routing and queuing, observability and billing pipelines. Does NOT cover application-level business logic outages (see customer-facing product runbooks).

text:
## Purpose
This page defines the **perf-canary gates** and **dashboards** used to validate the Hardware Tuning Profiles Pack during:
1) **Pre-merge (CI)**: prevent known regressions from landing.
2) **Post-deploy (staged rollout)**: catch production-only failures (fleet variance, noisy neighbors, real traffic mix) and provide fast rollback signals.

The gates are designed to be:
- **Safe-by-default**: strict on correctness/stability and tail latency.
- **Measurable and repeatable**: pinned workload matrix + standardized summary artifact.
- **Profile-aware**: thresholds differ by profile intent (e.g., H100 balanced vs throughput-first; B200 experimental).

> Wording note: these are **engineering validation gates** and define “expected behavior” for rollouts. They are not customer SLAs.

---

## Where these gates run
### Pre-merge (CI)
- System: **perf-canary**
- Trigger:
 - Required on...

title: Demo Tenant Reset Runbook (GTM Sales Demos)
preview:
## Purpose
This runbook describes the standard, repeatable procedure to reset the Redwood **sales demo tenant** (“demo org”) to a known-good state.

A correct reset accomplishes all of the following:
- Resets mutable state (test projects/routes/evals, demo app artifacts) to the standard baseline.
- Re-seeds **known-...
text:
## Purpose
This runbook describes the standard, repeatable procedure to reset the Redwood **sales demo tenant** (“demo org”) to a known-good state.

A correct reset accomplishes all of the following:
- Resets mutable state (test projects/routes/evals, demo app artifacts) to the standard baseline.
- Re-seeds **known-good demo scenarios** (projects, routes, prompt sets, eval fixtures).
- Rotates demo API keys (least-privilege, audited) and safely revokes old keys.
- Re-populates Console dashboards via synthetic traffic (cost/latency/errors/rollouts) so demos don’t start with empty graphs.
- Validates demo route **model pins** and **safe fallback policy**.

This procedure is designed to be fast (minutes) and safe (guardrails prevent impacting non-demo tenants).

---

## Audience
- Sales Engineering (SE) running live demos
- Eng Platform on-call supporting demo enablement
- SRE/Infra resp...

## Summary
This page specifies the v1 dashboards and alerting for **Optimize Quality Gates** (gating required before applying batching/caching/quantization profile changes).

The goal is to provide:
- A **gate-run funnel** view (proposed → running → passed/failed/overridden)
- Fast identification of **where failures are concentrated** (config type, model family, region, account tier, prompt set)

- **Drill-down paths** to gate artifacts (eval reports, prompt-set diffs, logs/traces) to reduce time-to-triage

## Purpose
This page describes how Redwood’s sales demo environment (“demo tenant”) is structured and how it is provisioned in a repeatable, safe way. It is intended for:
- Eng Platform and SRE (environment ownership, reliability, guardrails)

The goal: a production-like tenant with stable, known-good scenarios that can be reset in minutes, remains resilient to model catalog changes, and is safe to use for external screen shares.

---

## High-level architecture
The demo environment is a dedicated Redwood org (“demo org”) with a set of pre-created projects, routes, prompt sets/evals, synthetic usage pipelines, and least-privilege API keys. It depends on standard Redwood platform services, but is configured wit...

title: Cost Signal Fanout and Edge Actioning Playbook
preview:
Summary

This playbook describes the design, operational runbook, dashboard mappings, and rollout plan for the Cost Signal Fanout (CSF) system and its Edge Actioning component. CSF converts runtime cost telemetry into low-latency signals that inform per-request and per-tenant mitigation actions at the edge (API gate...
text:
Summary

This playbook describes the design, operational runbook, dashboard mappings, and rollout plan for the Cost Signal Fanout (CSF) system and its Edge Actioning component. CSF converts runtime cost telemetry into low-latency signals that inform per-request and per-tenant mitigation actions at the edge (API gateway, routing layer, or model selector). The goal is to keep unit economics predictable while preserving latency and quality SLOs.

Background and motivation

- Problem: spikes in per-token cost (e.g., due to unexpectedly long sequences, model cascade fallbacks, or noisy tenants) create unpredictable spend and SLA tension. Traditional billing alerts are slow and coarse-grained.
- Objective: provide sub-second cost-aware decisions that can 1) reduce incremental cost without violating latency SLOs, 2) protect core tenants from noisy neighbors, and 3) provide observability and ...

title: Routing Policy Failover Controller — Operational Runbook
preview:
## Purpose
This runbook covers day-to-day operations for the **Routing Policy Failover Controller** ("failover-controller"): enabling/disabling automation, using **dry-run** mode, handling **approval workflows**, performing **rollbacks**, and troubleshooting.

The controller evaluates region-level impairment signals...
text:
## Purpose
This runbook covers day-to-day operations for the **Routing Policy Failover Controller** ("failover-controller"): enabling/disabling automation, using **dry-run** mode, handling **approval workflows**, performing **rollbacks**, and troubleshooting.

The controller evaluates region-level impairment signals (reachability, error burn, tail latency burn, capacity exhaustion) and (optionally) executes routing policy changes to shift traffic away from impacted regions while preventing oscillation and overload.

## Scope
**In scope**
- Hosted API + Dedicated routing policy flips across regions
- Controller operation modes (disabled / dry-run / execute with approvals / execute fully automated)
- Anti-flap behavior (hold-down timers, rate limits)
- Rollback and stabilization workflows
- Auditability and incident-bot integration

**Out of scope**
- Customer-specific Private deploymen...

text:
## Overview
This page specifies the dashboards and alert rules used to detect, validate, and troubleshoot regional impairments and automated failover for:
- **Hosted API** (public API gateway + routing + data plane)
- **Dedicated** (reserved pools + drain/evacuation workflow + routing constraints)

The intent is to make regional failover **fast, safe, and explainable**:
- Fast detection of real regional impairment (avoid false positives).
- Clear correlation across the canonical signal hierarchy (ADR-042).
- Operator confidence via corroborating signals and auditability.
- Alert hygiene (high signal/noise) and stable thresholds.

This spec is written for SRE on-call, routing/control-plane engineers, and infra/network responders.

---

## Data sources and conventions
### Primary data sources
- **Metrics:** Prometheus/Thanos (prod + staging), 10s–30s scrape depending on service.
- **Log...
